feed.title: SAP Community - SAP S/4HANA
feed.subtitle: SAP S/4HANA blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: SAP Commodity Management: Driving the Bottom Line with Precision Expense Control
entry.content:
In commodity trading, third-party expenses are not merely overheads; they are critical factors that significantly influence the profitability of trading activities. Costs such as demurrage, brokerage, throughput charges, insurance, and tank cleaning, when not managed precisely, can lead to margin and revenue leakage. While SAP’s native capabilities offer robust expense management, the unique and dynamic requirements of commodity supply chains, including the need to track costs throughout the entire deal and contract lifecycle, necessitate a specialized approach.

To address these industry-specific challenges, we are thrilled to have launched SAP Commodity Expense Management, as part of S/4HANA 2025. This new product is purpose-built as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), designed specifically for commodity organizations to provide focused, industry-relevant expense management, all supported by dedicated and intuitive Fiori Apps delivering enhanced usability.

The solution delivers several key benefits:

  • Increased Automation: Leverages automation rules defined in Expense Master Data, automatic defaulting of expenses during planning and automated accrual reversals and adjustments.
  • Enhanced Integration and Visibility: Provides cross-company visibility of expenses across multiple document types from capture to settlement.
  • Improved Data Accuracy: Reduces the risk of margin and revenue leakage through better expense planning and integrated reporting.

SAP Commodity Expense Management offers a comprehensive suite of capabilities, ensuring granular control and enhanced visibility over commodity-related costs across both Energy and Agri industries:

  • Expense Setup & Configuration: This feature provides tools to define the fundamental structure of your expenses. It allows for the setup of various expense types, units of measure (UoMs), currencies, and account mapping. Users can configure automation rules, mass update expense rates, and maintain essential posting assignments for both accrual and settlement processes. Furthermore, the solution offers extensibility with flexibility to define attributes for determining templates and expense rates, along with APIs for mass upload of these rates.
  • Expense Planning: Essential for proactive cost management, this capability supports both automatic and manual expense planning. Expenses can be planned during deal capture, contract creation, nomination, and Agricultural Contract Management  Application Document items, allowing for iterative expense updates throughout the process. The system facilitates defaulting of expenses based on predefined automation rules and allows tracking of overrides and manual additions at various stages. It offers the ability to determine expenses using customer-specific attributes during Deal Capture.
  • Expense Accrual: This function handles the recognition of liabilities for services received. Accruals can be created and adjusted based on key events such as Deal Publish, Contract Approval, Nomination Confirmation, and ACM Application Documents/Ticketing. It includes features for automatic accrual reversals, automatic accrual on ticket corrections, and the ability to split and collate accruals for complex scenarios. Accrual postings are integrated during delivery execution.
  • Expense Settlement: Designed to streamline the payment process, this feature supports invoice verification and the settlement of both planned and unplanned expenses, distinct from core commodity contract settlements. It allows for correction of service provider and invoice amounts and provides for the recovery of expenses from contract or deal counterparties. An API is available to capture Expense Settlement, and it includes tax code determination for accurate financial postings. Settlement postings are integrated into the accounting process.

By bringing precision, automation, and comprehensive visibility to third-party costs, SAP Commodity Expense Management empowers organizations to transform expenses from a potential liability into a strategic advantage. Don’t let unmanaged costs erode your profitability; embrace the future of commodity expense control. We invite you to explore the capabilities further by visiting the SAP Help Portal and Roadmap Explorer (links below).

Visit SAP Help Portal for more an Administration Guide: https://help.sap.com/docs/CCS/7b6b503b6ae0446fb3d6801c98f56b1e/2fe3a2ae04e74a598c8c39f73b4710e3.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.title: Calculating IBP Technical Week “Key Figure Date” in S/4HANA Using CDS View
entry.content:
When extracting data from S/4HANA into SAP IBP, a standard calendar week may span across two months.
IBP splits such weeks into “technical weeks”, and many key figures require the first date of the technical week.
while working on custom IBP integration for transactional data (key fugures), I discovered that S/4HANA does not provide this date natively.
So I created a very simple CDS view to derive it using the I_CalendarDate standard view.
 
Example week:
  • 29-Apr-2024 → 03-May-2024
  • Crosses April → May
  • IBP interprets this as:
    • Technical Week A: 29–30 April → Key Figure Date: 29-Apr
    • Technical Week B: 01–03 May → Key Figure Date: 01-May

The CDS view below calculates this value automatically for any date.

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'IBP : Get Key Figure Date for any Date'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_IBP_Key_Figure_Date
  as select from I_CalendarDate
{
  key CalendarDate,

  @EndUserText.label:     'Key figure Date'
  @EndUserText.quickInfo: 'First day of the IBP Technical Week'
  case
    when cast( WeekDay as abap.int4 ) < cast( CalendarDay as abap.int4 )
    then dats_add_days( CalendarDate, -cast( WeekDay as abap.int4 ) + 1, 'INITIAL')
    else dats_add_days( CalendarDate, -cast( CalendarDay as abap.int4 ) + 1, 'INITIAL')
  end as KeyFigureDate
}

Result :

 

This CDS view can be used for :

  • Custom IBP extractors (CPI‑DS or RFC-based)
  • Time-based aggregation logic in Custom CDS views for IBP

Hope this will be helpful.

Regards,

Bharat Bajaj

entry.title: From Conversational AI to Business AI: Why SAP Joule Changes Enterprise Process Execution
entry.content:
Generative AI has quickly moved from experimentation to enterprise boardroom discussions. But for SAP consultants, architects, and transformation leaders, the real question is not whether AI can generate content.
The real question is:
Can AI understand enterprise processes, respect governance, and execute transactions within complex ERP landscapes?
That is where SAP Joule changes the conversation.
Joule is SAP’s generative AI agent embedded across the SAP suite of applications including S/4HANA, Ariba, SuccessFactors, and SAP BTP. It is designed specifically for enterprise business processes rather than general-purpose interaction.
Unlike other generative AI tools, Joule is not simply a chatbot on top of ERP. It is an embedded enterprise process agent, designed to work seamlessly within SAP’s architecture, data model, and governance framework.

1. Process-Aware, Not Just Prompt-Aware
Joule understands structured SAP business objects such as:
• Purchase Orders
• Invoices
• Journal Entries
• Sales Orders
It operates within live transactional contexts and respects user roles and authorizations. For architects, this means AI aligned with process design—not disconnected automation.

2. Native Enterprise Data Integration
Joule leverages SAP master and transactional data directly within the system landscape. It understands:
• Organizational hierarchies
• Role-based authorizations
• Process dependencies
This ensures accuracy, consistency, and alignment with enterprise architecture principles.

3. Governance by Design
For transformation leaders, trust is critical. Enterprise AI must meet compliance and security requirements.
Joule:
• Adheres to SAP’s data residency and privacy standards
• Operates within existing authorization frameworks
• Does not use customer business data to train foundation models for other tenants
Embedded governance ensures safe and compliant AI adoption.

4. AI That Acts — Not Just Advises
Joule moves AI from advisory to execution.
It can:
• Trigger workflows
• Draft journal entries
• Create purchase requisitions
• Guide users through process steps
This is where AI starts delivering tangible business outcomes rather than just insights.

5. Cross-Functional Process Orchestration
Enterprise transformation requires breaking functional silos.
Joule works across S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and SAP BTP:
• Retrieves HR data from SuccessFactors
• Processes financial transactions in S/4HANA
• Updates procurement records in Ariba
All within a single interaction. This end-to-end orchestration simplifies complex business processes.

6. No Training Required
Joule comes pre-trained on SAP business objects and processes:
• No need to teach it what a Purchase Order or General Ledger is
• Aligns with standard SAP process models
• Accelerates adoption without steep learning curves

7. Developer Enablement
Beyond end users, Joule supports developers by:
• Understanding ABAP and SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model)
• Generating code aligned with SAP Clean Core principles
• Supports innovation without compromising upgrade stability
This accelerates transformation initiatives while preserving system integrity.

Joule goes beyond traditional generative AI chatbots. Embedded within SAP applications, it serves as the user interface for Business AI, simplifying and orchestrating interactions and workflows across complex enterprise environments.

For consultants, architects and transformation leaders, the strategic takeaway is clear:
AI value in SAP landscapes will not come from conversations alone.
It will come from embedded, governed, and process-aware intelligence that drives measurable outcomes.
Joule represents a foundational step toward that future.

How are you approaching embedded AI adoption in your SAP landscape—and what challenges are you seeing in moving from pilot use cases to enterprise-wide execution?
We would love to hear your experiences, insights, and lessons learned.

Source:
SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help
Discovery Center

Other Blogs:

System Conversion to SAP S/4HANA - SAP Community

entry.title: How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End
entry.content:
One of the most powerful capabilities in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) is its ability to automate invoice processing the moment a customer or vendor sends an email. With SAP Document Information Extraction (Document AI) integrated into ESM, the journey from a raw invoice PDF to a validated posting in SAP S/4HANA Private or Public Edition becomes almost fully automated.
Click on Image to view High Quality
Click on Image to view High Quality

Step 1: The User Sends an Email with an Invoice

A customer or vendor sends an email to the SAP ESM dedicated email address configured for automatic case creation.

The invoice may be:

  • Attached as a PDF, JPEG, PNG, or
  • Embedded in the email body itself

SAP ESM picks up both.

Step 2: SAP ESM Automatically Creates a Case

Once the email reaches the ESM mailbox:

  • A new case is created automatically
  • The email body and all attachments are stored within the case
  • The attachments (PDFs, images) trigger SAP Document AI processing immediately

This eliminates manual case creation entirely.

Step 3: SAP Document AI Processes the Invoice

SAP Document AI retrieves the attachment from the case and begins extraction using OCR and machine learning.

It identifies:

  • Invoice number
  • Customer or vendor name
  • Address
  • Line items
  • Totals & taxes
  • Payment terms
  • Contract or PO references

Document AI also calculates a confidence score for each extracted field.
These scores help determine whether the data is reliable enough to auto‑post or requires user intervention.

Step 4: Validation Using Business Rules & Customer Framework

Once extraction is complete, SAP ESM applies:

  • Business rules
  • Customer validation frameworks
  • Mapping logic
  • Master data checks

These validations are essential before communicating with S/4HANA.

Examples:

  • Is the customer known in S/4?
  • Is the invoice number valid and not duplicated?
  • Does the PO exist?
  • Do line item totals match?

Only when the validations pass does ESM proceed to the next step.

Step 5: Invoice Number & Customer Auto‑Identification

If Document AI finds the invoice number, SAP ESM updates the line items and case fields:

  • invoiceNumber → updated
  • customerNumber → auto‑identified
  • isClearing → set according to logic (e.g., invoice found, ready to post)

This gives the ESM user immediate visibility:

  • Has the system recognized the invoice?
  • Has the customer been identified?
  • Is the invoice ready for posting?

Step 6: ESM Sends the Validated Invoice to S/4HANA via OData APIs

Once the case has all mandatory and validated data, the ESM user can trigger the next action:

“Post Invoice to S/4”

At this point:

  • ESM calls S/4HANA OData APIs
  • A draft or final FI invoice is created
  • Additional steps like clearing, matching, or workflow approvals occur inside S/4

This hands-off process ensures:

  • No manual entry in S/4
  • No data inconsistencies
  • Full auditability from source → document → posting

Step 7: ESM User Monitors and Completes the Process

Inside the ESM case, the user can now see:

  • Status of the posting
  • Document number returned from S/4
  • Any errors (e.g., master data inconsistencies)
  • New actions available for follow-up

They can then:

  • Update the customer
  • Add missing reference information
  • Re-trigger the posting
  • Close the case once processing is complete

Result: A Fully Streamlined, AI‑Driven Invoice Workflow

By integrating SAP ESM, Document AI, and S/4HANA, organizations benefit from:

✔ Zero manual invoice entry

✔ Auto-identification of customers and invoice data

✔ Early error detection using business rules

✔ Faster posting to S/4HANA

✔ End-to-end transparency in one central case

✔ Higher accuracy and reduced effort

This modernizes invoice handling from a traditionally manual process into an intelligent, automated workflow.

Learning Journey :  Business Documents in ESM

entry.title: Using Adapter HTTP - Response 403 for OData API Post Method- How to solve the big challenge
entry.content:
Connecting SAP S/4 HANA or ERP systems with external applications using OData services is becoming the backbone of modern enterprise integrations. Tools such as SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) or Integration Suite’s using HTTP Adapter make it easy to consume or expose APIs—until you hit a frustrating roadblock:

“HTTP 403 – Forbidden” when calling an OData API POST method.

This error is notoriously deceptive. Your GET requests work fine. Your endpoint is correct. Your payload is correct. Yet POST still fails.
Why?

In this blog, we’ll break down why HTTP 403 happens, what makes POST requests special, and—most importantly—how to solve this challenge with proven, real-world fixes.

1. Understanding the 403 Forbidden Error

A 403 Forbidden response means:

The server understood your request but refuses to authorize it.

In the context of OData POST calls via the HTTP adapter, this almost always indicates an authorization, CSRF, or policy-related issue.

2. Why GET Works but POST Fails

Many developers test an OData service with a GET call, find that it works, and assume the setup is correct. But POST behaves differently because it:

  • Modifies data, triggering stricter authorization checks
  • Requires CSRF token handling (depending on the endpoint)
  • May require additional headers

This is why GET succeeding does not prove the integration is fully configured.

3. The Top Causes of HTTP 403 for OData POST (and How to Fix Them)

Cause 1 — Missing or Incorrect CSRF Token

Many SAP OData services require a valid X-CSRF-Token for POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE.

✔ Solution:
Perform a GET request with header:

X-CSRF-Token: Fetch

and capture:

  • Returned CSRF token
  • Returned cookies

Then send them along in your POST call:

X-CSRF-Token: <token>
Cookie: <cookies>

Cause 2 — Incorrect or Missing Headers

Some OData services enforce additional constraints.

Mandatory headers may include:

Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json

4. How to Troubleshoot 403 Efficiently

Step 1 — Test POST via Postman

  • Fetch CSRF token first
  • Send cookies + token in POST
  • Add all required headers

Step 2 — Check SAP Gateway Error Logs

Use transaction /IWFND/ERROR_LOG or /IWBEP/ERROR_LOG.

You may see:

  • "No CREATE authorization"
  • "CSRF Token validation failed"
  • "Request method not allowed"

These messages are extremely helpful.

5. Example Adapter HTTP Configuration for POST (CPI)

Headers 

Method: POST
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json
X-CSRF-Token: <fetched token>
Cookie: <SAP cookies>
Body (Example)
{
  "Name": "Sample Item",
  "Quantity": 10
}

Set Header for Cookie + CSRF token 

 Groovy Script 1(Shown from above Iflow image)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;


def Message processData(Message message) {
    def headers = message.getHeaders();
    def cookie = headers.get("Set-Cookie");
    def csrf = headers.get("x-csrf-token");
    StringBuffer bufferedCookie = new StringBuffer();
    for (Object item : cookie)
    {
        bufferedCookie.append(item + "; ");
    }
    message.setHeader("Cookie", bufferedCookie.toString());
    message.setHeader("x-csrf-token", csrf)

    return message
}

POST Method  

Session handling

On Exchange: If this configuration option is selected, the session will be re-used for the whole processing of one message, meaning for one message exchange. This makes sense if the integration flow executes several calls to the same backend during one message exchange, for example in case of a loop or multicast scenario. Then the subsequent calls are much faster because no authentication is to be executed. It can also be used if the server keeps a state and you want to connect several times to the stateful service within one exchange

6. Conclusion – Turning a Frustrating 403 into a Successful POST

The HTTP 403 Forbidden error for OData POST calls is one of the most common yet misunderstood issues when integrating SAP systems. But once you understand:

  • CSRF handling
  • Role/authorization requirements
  • Header and policy rules
  • Gateway restrictions

…the problem becomes easy to solve.

Following the troubleshooting guidelines above will help you quickly identify the root cause and ensure your POST requests work reliably—whether you're integrating via CPI, API Management, BTP, or direct HTTP calls.

entry.title: NEW RELEASE: Mapping the Journey to SAP S/4HANA - A practical guide for senior IT leadership
entry.content:
What is SAP’s Mapping Your Journey to SAP S/4HANA?

SAP’s Mapping Your Journey to SAP S/4HANA is a strategic, experience-based guide designed to help senior leaders, IT decision-makers, and project managers plan to justify and execute their transition from legacy SAP ERP systems to the next-generation SAP Cloud ERP or Business Suite. Drawing on insights from thousands of real transformation projects, the guide blends high-level strategic choices with actionable implementation guidance and practical tool recommendations.

At its core, this edition shows how SAP Cloud ERP is not just a technical upgrade but a cornerstone for broader digital transformation, one that can unlock future-ready business models, intelligence-enabled processes, and improved operational agility.

 

More Than a Technical Upgrade

For many organizations, the move to SAP S/4HANA represents one of the most significant transformation initiatives of the decade. Yet the most successful customers do not approach it as a simple system upgrade. They see it as an opportunity to modernize their enterprise core, simplify operations, and position their business for continuous innovation.

SAP’s Mapping Your Journey to SAP S/4HANA provides practical, experience-based guidance to help organizations navigate this transformation with confidence. Built on insights from thousands of customer projects across industries, the framework helps business and IT leaders make informed decisions, align stakeholders, and turn complexity into clarity.

At its heart, the journey to SAP Cloud ERP is about creating a future-ready foundation to compete in a rapidly changing world.

Start with Strategy: Aligning Business Ambition and Technology

A successful SAP Cloud ERP journey begins with a clear understanding of why the transformation matters to the business.

Rather than focusing solely on technical drivers, leading organizations align their S/4HANA roadmap with strategic priorities such as:

  • Improving end-to-end process efficiency and transparency
  • Enabling faster, data-driven decision-making
  • Supporting new business models and digital services
  • Reducing technical debt and operational complexity

SAP Cloud ERP, powered by SAP HANA’s in-memory technology, simplifies data models and enables real-time analytics directly within business processes. Combined with a modern user experience and embedded intelligence, it creates a platform designed to evolve alongside the business, and not to constrain it.

Choosing the Right Path: One Size Does Not Fit All

Every organization’s starting point is different. SAP recognizes this and offers multiple transition paths to SAP Cloud ERP, allowing customers to choose the approach that best fits their goals, risk profile, and timeline.

System Conversion: Ideal for customers who want to retain existing processes and configurations while moving to SAP Cloud ERP. This approach minimizes disruption and accelerates time to value, making it attractive for organizations seeking continuity with modernization.

New Implementation: Designed for customers ready to reimagine their business processes. By adopting standard best practices and reducing historical complexity, this approach enables greater simplification and long-term agility.

Selective Data Transition: A hybrid approach that allows customers to selectively move data, processes, or organizational units. It offers flexibility for complex landscapes, phased transformations, or mergers and divestitures.

The right choice depends on business ambition, regulatory requirements, and readiness for change, not just technical considerations.

 

Cloud as a Strategic Enabler

For many customers, the journey to SAP Cloud ERP goes hand in hand with a broader cloud strategy. SAP S/4HANA Cloud options enable organizations to shift from infrastructure management to innovation, benefiting from:

  • Scalability and operational resilience
  • Faster change management to adopt innovations
  • Predictable lifecycle management

Whether deployed on-premise, in the cloud, or in a hybrid model, SAP Cloud ERP provides the flexibility to evolve at a pace that aligns with business needs.

 

Laying the Foundation for Success

Technology alone does not deliver transformation. The guide highlights several critical success factors that consistently differentiate successful projects.

People, Skills, and Governance: Transformation requires strong sponsorship, cross-functional collaboration, and early investment in skills. Clear governance and change management ensure that transformation delivers lasting business impact, far beyond a technical go live.

Process Simplification and a Clean Core: SAP encourages customers to embrace standardization where it adds value and to reduce unnecessary custom code. A clean core approach lowers total cost of ownership, accelerates upgrades, and keeps the system ready for innovation.

Data and Custom Code Readiness: High-quality data is essential for intelligent processes. Preparing master data, archiving obsolete information, and analyzing custom code early reduces risk and avoids delays during migration.

 

Turning Insight into Action with SAP Tools

SAP provides a comprehensive set of tools and accelerators that support customers throughout their transformation journey, from early discovery to execution.

  • SAP Signavio solutions help customers understand how their current processes perform and where SAP Cloud ERP innovations can drive measurable improvement.
  • SAP Readiness Check offers a fact-based view of technical readiness, highlighting simplification items, add-on compatibility, and required adaptations.
  • Migration and planning tools help structure the transformation roadmap, ensuring transparency and predictability.

Together, these tools enable customers to make decisions based on data.


Conclusion: Building the Intelligent, Sustainable Enterprise

The journey to SAP Cloud ERP is a pivotal moment that goes beyond modernizing ERP, but to rethink how the enterprise operates and creates value.

With a clear strategy, the right transition path, and a strong focus on people and processes, organizations can turn transformation into a competitive advantage. SAP’s proven methodologies, tools, and ecosystem help customers move forward with confidence accelerating innovation while reducing risks.

Ultimately, SAP Cloud ERP is not the destination. It is the foundation for becoming an intelligent, sustainable enterprise — ready to adapt, innovate, and grow in the years ahead.

The document can be accessed via sap.com(AEM) or as PDF in the Digital Library

entry.title: Meet Your Ops Agents - Powered by SAP Automation Pilot and Joule
entry.content:
Building on our latest innovation around SAP Automation Pilot and AI-powered IT Operations, I am excited to share that we are already seeing real productive use cases for Day-2 Operations Agents which are powered by SAP Automation Pilot content and consumed directly in Joule in a truly agentic manner.

🎥  Meet Your Ops Agent

See an example about Ops Agent powered by SAP Automation Pilot and discover how easy it is to set up an MCP server, expose automations, and trigger them in an agentic way through Joule.

 

This approach unlocks a completely new way to ops  automation  where AI agents can reason, execute, and orchestrate operational tasks seamlessly following customers requests and needs.

The demonstration walks through a scenario about an S/4HANA Health Monitoring Ops Agent in Joule, designed to:

  • Provide system health & diagnostics insights;
  • Perform automated analysis & deliver actionable recommendations;
  • Proactively send alerts when potential issues are detected;

📖Just the beginning

 There is an unlimited range of Ops Agent scenarios enabled through SAP Automation Pilot, including for example:

  • SAP BTP Operations (e.g. Cloud Foundry applications,  SAP HANA Cloud, SAP cTMS, etc.);
  • SAP Build and Day 2 Operations for your apps;
  • SAP Integration Suite checks and actions via automated API calls; 
  • Integrations with custom solutions;
  • And many more …

Stay tuned as we will be revealing more real-life agentic operations scenarios in the coming weeks.

🚀 Get involved: Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative

If you would like to bring your own automation scenario and experience first-hand the AI-driven operations capabilities of SAP Automation Pilot and Joule, join our Customer Engagement Initiative: Agentic AI Operations with SAP Automation Pilot

⏳Registration is open until March 15, 2026 - secure your spot soon!

 

Learn More

If you would like to explore MCP servers in more detail or start using them in your own landscapes, please check out the SAP Automation Pilot official documentation which provides further insights:

  • MCP Server Overview;
  • Managing MCP Servers;
  • Integrating SAP Automation Pilot with Joule Studio ;

Looking forward to collaborating and shaping the future of agentic IT operations together.

entry.title: Unlocking Strategic Pricing with SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine
entry.content:
In today’s global markets, commodities are no longer just raw materials—they’re strategic assets. Volatile prices, complex contracts, and multiple market indices make commodity management a high-stakes challenge for companies in energy, agriculture, metals and manufacturing. Enter SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine a core component of SAP Commodity Management, providing a powerful tool designed to simplify, automate, and optimize commodity pricing.

What is SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine?

The Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine is a sophisticated module in SAP that enables companies to calculate commodity prices dynamically based on market indices, contracts, and predefined rules. Unlike static pricing methods, Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine can handle complex pricing formulas, variable discounts, secondary cost adjustments, and quality differentials, giving businesses a single source of truth for commodity costs.

Key highlights include:

  • Automated price calculations based on market benchmarks
  • Integration with SAP S/4HANA for seamless procurement, sales, and finance processes
  • Flexibility to handle diverse commodities like crude oil, refined fuel products, metals, grains, and chemical products
  • Drives commodity risk exposure to facilitate risk management and mitigation

Why Businesses Need Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

Commodity price fluctuations are unpredictable. Companies that rely on manual pricing processes face several challenges:

  • Risk of errors and inconsistencies across regions
  • Delays in price updates, leading to financial losses
  • Difficulty in tracking contract obligations against market movements

Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine addresses these challenges by:

  1. Standardizing pricing logic across all commodities
  2. Reducing operational complexity with automated calculations
  3. Enabling proactive risk management by linking prices to market indices
  4. Enhancing transparency for audit and compliance purposes

Core Features of Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

  1. Dynamic Price Formulas
    Configure formulas that automatically calculate prices using market indices, secondary charges, and quality adjustments.
  2. Index-Based Pricing
    Link your contracts to global benchmarks (e.g., Brent crude, LME metals, CME wheat futures) for real-time market-reflective pricing.
  3. Flexible Adjustments
    Incorporate location-specific logistics, quality differentials, and taxes into price calculations automatically.
  4. Scenario Simulation
    Run “what-if” scenarios to assess the impact of price fluctuations on margins and contracts before they occur.  
  5. Seamless Integration
    Works with SAP S/4HANA’s procurement, sales, finance, and logistics modules, ensuring a unified view of commodity costs across the e2e supply chain.

What’s new with Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

The new Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine offers the following enhancements:

  • A new RAP business object R_CmmdtyPrcgConditionFormulaTP for formula data model and a new RAP business object R_CmmdtyPrcgPriceFixationSetTP for price fixation groups of future basis pricing process have been included.
  • A new CPE interface CL_CMM_CPE_INTERFACE_BO allows you to update CPE data in batch scenarios through BAPI.
  • Changes to the allocation data can be made manually through the allocation workbench (CMM_ALLOC). With this functionality, you will now be able to switch the mode from automatic allocation to manual.
  • Price quotations are now visible in display mode for logistic documents. This allows you to revisit the quotations from the last time it was updated.
  • All the pricing aspects in logistic documents are now saved locally within the document. With this functionality, the changes are isolated to the specific document.
  • Optimized Future/Basis Pricing scenarios.
  • Considerable performance improvement for mass processing scenarios with high data volume.

Benefits of Implementing Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

  • New strategic engine: where all new/enhanced capabilities and innovations would be incorporated in the future
  • Enhanced Transactional data consistency: Configuration changes in formula do not impact existing contracts using defaulted formula details.
  • Operational Efficiency: Automates complex pricing, reducing manual intervention and errors.
  • Financial Accuracy: Real-time market pricing ensures accurate P&L reporting and cost allocation.
  • Strategic Advantage: Quickly adapt to market changes, optimizing procurement, hedging, and sales strategies.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Transparent pricing formulas and audit trails help meet internal and external reporting requirements.
  • Clean Core: Standard extensibility options such as key user and developer extensibility is supported with the introduction of the RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) data model.
  • Cloud APIs: RAP re-use component offers cloud consumable APIs.
  • Increased end user satisfaction: due to enhanced performance and system response time.

Who Should Use Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine?

Companies dealing with:

  • Energy Commodities: Oil, refined products, biofuels
  • Agriculture & Consumer Products: Grains, cocoa, coffee, sugar
  • Metals & Mining: Base metals, precious metals, concentrates, coal
  • Chemicals & Raw Materials: Fertilizers, polymers, specialty chemicals

If your business deals with high-volume commodities, dynamic market conditions, or complex contracts, Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine can transform the way you manage pricing.

Conclusion - a powerful tool designed to simplify, automate, and optimize commodity pricing.

In volatile commodity markets, price accuracy isn’t just a convenience—it’s a competitive advantage. SAP Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine empowers organizations to calculate, automate, and optimize commodity prices while integrating seamlessly into existing SAP workflows.

By adopting Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine, businesses can reduce operational risk, improve financial transparency, and stay agile in the face of market fluctuations—turning commodity pricing from a challenge into a strategic tool.

For more information please follow this link to help.sap.com: help.sap.com - Advanced Commodity Pricing Engine

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 FPS01: Final What's New & Product Assistance
entry.content:
With immediate effect, the final version of What's New information and Product Assistance for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 FPS01 is now available:

  • To access the final version of the Product Assistance on the current SAP Help Portal pages, please use SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.
  • To access the final What’s New information, please use What's New Viewer for SAP S/4HANA or What's New Viewer for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

For any feedback you may have, you can use the commenting feature for What's New. With this feature, authors can evaluate your feedback and incorporate it into the documentation, if needed. We hope that by opening this channel of communication, we can learn how to further improve our What’s New content for you. To use this feature, you need to be logged into the SAP Help Portal. The feature is only available in the English version of the documents. To learn more about how the feedback process works in SAP Help Portal, see Giving Documentation Feedback.

In addition, you have access to further content, for example, the conversion and upgrade guides specifically tailored for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Please refer to the SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition pages for more details

entry.title: Why SELECT returns different rows when querying a CDS View on MSEG versus MSEG directly
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on MSEG
  • ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG
  • Why the Row Count Differs
  • Key Takeaways

 

Introduction

If you’ve recently worked with S/4HANA and created a custom CDS view on a standard table such as MSEG (*), you may have noticed something surprising:

Even if the CDS view is a simple SELECT FROM MSEG without any WHERE clause and there's no CDS Access Control implied, the number of rows returned by ABAP SELECT * FROM ZCDS_E_VIEW may not match the results of ABAP SELECT * FROM MSEG.

(*) You may find other tables like MSEG in the note 2206980 - Material Inventory Managment: change of data model in S/4HANA - SAP for Me.

 

Custom CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_V_MSEG'
define view ZCDS_E_MSEG as select from MSEG
{
  *
}

NB: a DDIC-based CDS view is used here to make the example simple, but as it's obsolete since ABAP 7.57 CDS view entities should be used instead.

 

ABAP SELECT on MSEG

SELECT * FROM MSEG
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_mseg).
This example assumes there are some lines in MSEG for MBLNR = '5000000000'.
This ABAP SELECT may return more rows than the below ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG.
 

ABAP SELECT on ZCDS_E_MSEG

SELECT * FROM zcds_e_mseg
WHERE mblnr = '5000000000'
INTO TABLE (lt_cds_mseg).
Even though this query selects all rows from the CDS view ZCDS_E_MSEG (which is based on the physical MSEG table without any WHERE), the number of rows may be fewer than the ABAP SELECT on MSEG.
 

Why the Row Count Differs

  1. The table MSEG exists physically in the database but is no longer maintained

    • In S/4HANA, the real material document data lives in MATDOC.

    • The legacy table MSEG still exists in the database with the original ECC data, but is no more updated by S/4HANA transactions.

  2. ABAP SELECT on MSEG is redirected to the replacement object

    • When ABAP code does SELECT * FROM MSEG, SAP automatically redirects the query to the CDS replacement view NSDM_E_MSEG, which reads MATDOC.



      NB: NSDM_E_MSEG filters MATDOC using WHERE record_type = 'MDOC' but it's not implied in the problem discussed here.
      You can see in the SQL Trace (ST05) how ABAP SELECT ... FROM MSEG ... is automatically using the replacement object:

    • This redirection ensures that old programs continue to work correctly and return all material document lines, i.e. those lines migrated and updated in S/4HANA live.

  3. Custom CDS views on MSEG read the physical table directly

    • Any CDS view defined on MSEG does not use the replacement object, so it reads the actual content of the MSEG table, which are the data migrated from ECC, but are no more updated in S/4HANA.

    • If the table MSEG is empty in your system, it's because the Obsolete Data Handling tool has run (since S/4HANA 2023, it's the transaction code SODH, which has superseded ODH_DATA_PROCESSING). Reference: SAP S/4HANA Upgrade Guide, section Cleaning Up Obsolete Data After the Upgrade.

 

✅Key Takeaways

  • Do not rely on MSEG for new development; always use MATDOC or NSDM replacement views such as NSDM_E_MSEG.

  • NEVER use the old tables in your custom CDS views because they contain obsolete data.

  • After you have successfully tested and validated the upgrade results, you can clean up the obsolete data with the Obsolete Data Handling tool, to free space and to avoid the confusion and panic among developers.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1: What's in it for the Utilities Industry?
entry.content:
The first feature pack (FPS1) of the SAP S/4HANA 2025 has just been released to customers. Below is an overview of its key innovations tailored specifically for the utilities industry:

1. Enterprise search for utilities master data
Using the Enterprise Search from Fiori Launchpad, you can now search various utilities-specific master data - such as premise, utility installation, point-of-delivery, device and device information record. Starting from the search results,  you can directly access frequently used apps and transactions for each master data entity. This intuitive approach significantly reduces search time and enhances operational efficiency. 

2. Graphical display of utilities interval data
A new Fiori app Display Utilities Time Series Values (F8929) is available for SAP S/4HANA Utilities, designed for the Meter Data Specialist (Utilities) role. This app delivered detailed insights into time series values from EDM profiles through a graphical representation that combines the values with related status information.

3. Archiving of utility installations and premises and deletion of unused device information records 
New archiving object ISU_EANL is now available to archive utility installations, including their related installation structure and points-of-delivery. Additionally, the archiving object ISU_EVBC enables the archiving of premises. Especially the first one (ISU_EANL) comprises comprehensive validations for dependent data, making sure that all related documents and master data entities are archived beforehand. Furthermore, a new report REG_DEV_INFO_REC_CLEANUP, has been introduced to delete unused device information records. 

4. More flexible completeness check for profile values
Based on customer feedback from the SAP Continuous Influence session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities, transaction EEDMPROFVALCHECK (Check Whether Profile Values Are Complete) has been enhanced:

  1. The meter reading unit and service type of the utility installation are now available as additional selection criteria.
  2. Existing BAdI ISU_BI_RTP_STATUS_CHECK method STATUS_CHECK_EXT now provides more precise information regarding the billability of profile values.

5. Additional CDS views for time series data

SAP S/4HANA Utilities now includes multiple CDS views for time series data extraction, supporting various interval lengths, status fields, and related attributes. For details on these new CDS views, refer to the What's New.

6. Enhancements in  SAP Market Process Management

The What's New  section in the SAP Help Portal outlines several enhancements related to SAP Market Process Management.  For full details, please refer to the following link: What's New . You can find utilities-related content in a dedicated section under Industries > Energy & Natural Resources.

Finally - here is a list of previous SAP Community blog posts, beginning with release SAP S/4HANA 2023:

 SAP S/4HANA release
(and feature pack)		Available
since		 
2023Oct 2023SAP S/4HANA 2023: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS1Feb 2024SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS1: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS2Oct 2024SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2023 FPS3Feb 2025SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS3: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
2025Oct 2025SAP S/4HANA 2025: What's in it for the Utilities Industry?

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: Where ORD Is Used in SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud & SAP Databricks
entry.content:
SAP Open Resource Discovery (ORD) is becoming a foundational standard across the SAP ecosystem because it provides a consistent, self‑describing metadata layer for APIs, events, business objects, and data products. This metadata standardization is what enables smooth interoperability across SAP’s modern data and integration platforms.

Below are the key places where ORD is used today — and how it empowers seamless integration, especially with SAP Datasphere, SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Databricks.

1. SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com)

SAP Business Accelerator Hub uses ORD as one of the primary mechanisms for publishing APIs and events. Any API showing an ORD ID on its overview page is officially ORD-enabled, meaning it exposes standardized metadata that can be consumed by SAP tools and data platforms.

This ensures:

  • Consistent API descriptions
  • Discoverability by SAP’s central aggregators
  • Compatibility with downstream systems like SAP Business Data Cloud
This is the first indicator that an API can participate in the broader data product ecosystem. 
 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)

SAP uses ORD internally to improve SAP-to-SAP integration and provide a unified metadata layer that development tools (like SAP Business Application Studio) can consume for enhanced API discovery and automation.

Each Product Owner from SAP Products LoB will be develop, design ORD to make it available in SAP Business Accelerator Hub (api.sap.com) and Data Products Marketplace for you..

3. SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)

SAP Business Data Cloud actively uses ORD to describe Data Products and make them discoverable to consumers across the SAP ecosystem, including SAP Datasphere. ORD helps BDC deliver a unified catalog with:

  • Descriptions of datasets
  • Relationships
  • Capabilities
  • Higher-level metadata structures like data products and business entities
In addition, third‑party systems such as SAP Databricks are required to describe data products using ORD before publishing them into SAP Business Data Cloud.
 
This ensures smooth catalog integration and consistent metadata across SAP and non-SAP data sources.
 

4. SAP Datasphere

SAP Datasphere consumes data products published to SAP Business Data Cloud — and because BDC requires ORD‑based metadata, Datasphere gains:

  • Automatically discoverable data products
  • Standardized metadata for modeling
  • Compatibility with external data such as Databricks Delta Lake

Datasphere uses ORD indirectly because it relies on BDC’s ORD-driven catalog for understanding and federating data assets.

Additionally, SAP’s hybrid integration patterns show that Datasphere can integrate with Databricks using JDBC or federated models — and ORD ensures data products between these systems are consistently described.

5. SAP Databricks (Enterprise Databricks Integration)

SAP Databricks integrates deeply with SAP Business Data Cloud using BDC Connect, enabling bi-directional sharing of data products. To publish a data product from SAP Databricks to SAP Business Data Cloud:

  • The metadata must be described using ORD and CSN (Core Schema Notation)
  • The data product becomes discoverable to SAP systems (e.g., Datasphere) via BDC

Furthermore, Databricks–SAP integration uses Delta Share, JDBC, and federated models, but ORD plays the critical role of standardizing data product metadata.

This creates a smooth, unified integration path:

SAP applications → ORD-based metadata → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere & Databricks

SAP Architecture Center also confirms this hybrid pattern where business data from SAP and non‑SAP (Databricks) is harmonized through Business Data Cloud and Datasphere, enabling interoperable AI/ML and analytics scenarios.

 Refer my SAP blog

6. Data Products Across the SAP Ecosystem

ORD also provides standardized metadata for:

  • Entity Types / Business Objects
  • APIs and Events
  • Data Products 
This consistency is the key enabler for cross‑platform interoperability — especially as Data Products become central to the SAP ecosystem via Business Data Cloud and Datasphere.

Summary: How ORD Enables Smooth Integration Across SAP & Databricks

With ORD in place:

  • APIs with an ORD ID on api.sap.com can be safely consumed to build data products in SAP Business Data Cloud.
  • Business Data Cloud uses ORD metadata to catalog and serve data products to SAP Datasphere and SAP Databricks.
  • SAP Databricks uses ORD when publishing its own data products back into Business Data Cloud.
  • SAP Datasphere leverages ORD-driven metadata to discover, model, and federate data products seamlessly.

ORD is the metadata “glue” that ensures smooth integration across:

SAP BTP → Business Accelerator Hub → Business Data Cloud → Datasphere → Databricks

And the more SAP and partner solutions adopt ORD, the smoother, more automated, and more scalable integrations become across the SAP landscape.

entry.title: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI) on https://influence.sap.com allow you, as an SAP customer or partner, to gain early insights into SAP's product developments and work directly with SAP teams to shape future product directions. This project presents a unique opportunity to work with SAPUI5 flexibility product managers and engineering teams, leveraging your expertise customizing SAPUI5 apps with key user adaptation.

Join the upcoming CEI project to make an impact on how key users interact with key user adaptation

If you’re already convinced – join here. If not, read on 🙂

What is key user adaptation?

Key user adaptation is a mode that allows extending SAPUI5 applications for other users right in the browser. It is highly intuitive and requires no technical or programming skills. Just use drag and drop to make UI changes – no modification or copy of SAP standard coding necessary! Read more here.

What is the targeted improvement?

Although the product is mature and widely used, we actively evolve it by delivering new functionality and capability enhancements alongside continuous user experience improvements, informed by UI testing and direct customer feedback. 

We are in the process of overhauling key user adaptation’s UI, simplifying the overall approach while making often-used scenarios more streamlined. We’ve taken a close look at the version history (of activated adaptations) and how our users interact with it, extended the “embed third-party content” feature, widened the scope of custom fields that can be added to the UI – and more.

To ensure that we are on the right way, we need your help!

Who should participate?

Users who want to adapt standard SAPUI5 apps for business users. Both key users and technical users are welcome.

Why should you participate?

  • Gain understanding of SAPUI5 flexibility
  • Chance to influence a central SAPUI5 feature
  • Early access to prototypes

How can you participate?

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to share your feedback and engage with SAPUI5 flexibility development teams. Register by March 15, 2026, and make a true impact on how you can customize SAP apps to your needs!

Click here to sign up for our project. (A short overview about how to get started on influence.sap.com can be found here).

After you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call where you will get more information. This call is followed by individual or grouped feedback sessions.

Thank you in advance for helping us shape the future of SAPUI5 flexibility!

entry.title: Running SAP AMS Like a Product, Not a Support Factory
entry.content:
Running SAP AMS Like a Product, Not a Support Factory

Introduction

In many S/4HANA environments, the real challenge begins after go-live, during stabilisation. This blog shares a practical experience from a healthcare SAP landscape where SLA compliance was strong, yet recurring issues were eroding business confidence. I will outline what changed when we shifted focus from ticket closure to process-level stability.

A few years ago, I was supporting a large healthcare and pharmaceutical organisation in India. The SAP landscape was not small. Multiple company codes, manufacturing plants, finance, MM, SD, and integration touchpoints. At peak, the AMS team was handling close to  70 to 90 incidents per week across modules.

On paper, we were stable. SLAs were green. Response times were acceptable. Escalations were limited.

But stability is not the same as confidence.

Three weeks after one S/4HANA go-live stabilisation cycle, finance users began reporting repeated FI posting failures during month-end close. The same issue was logged again. And again. And again.

Over a two-week period, the same posting issue surfaced repeatedly, close to 8 to 10 times, mostly during month-end processing.

Each ticket was resolved within SLA. Each one was technically “closed.”
And yet, nothing was actually fixed.

From a dashboard perspective, AMS looked healthy. From a business perspective, frustration was building.

I still remember one escalation call. A senior finance stakeholder asked:

“If we are closing tickets, why does this keep coming back during month-end?”

That question was uncomfortable. Because the honest answer was ,we were optimizing for closure, not stability.

What Was Really Happening

The root cause turned out to be a configuration gap combined with a background job timing conflict. Nothing dramatic. No major design flaw. Just a gap that slipped through during hypercare.

But the way we were operating AMS made it worse.

Incidents were assigned by queue. Functional would resolve one aspect. Technical would adjust something. The ticket would close. And ownership would dissolve.

Two weeks later, month-end would expose the same weakness.

We had documentation. We had RCA notes. What we did not have was single-point accountability for eliminating recurrence.

And that is where I realised this was not a technical issue. It was an operating model issue.

The Shift

We paused low-priority enhancement work for that module. Not popular at the time.

We grouped recurring incidents instead of treating them individually. Assigned one accountable lead for the entire FI process area. Not for a ticket for the outcome.

We introduced a simple stability metric: repeat incident count per process area. Not just SLA compliance.

Repeat FI posting incidents during month-end reduced by approximately 40 to 60% over the next two stabilisation cycles..

Total weekly incident volume across SAP reduced gradually as patterns were eliminated rather than recycled.

More importantly, escalation tone changed. Instead of: “Why is this failing again?”

The conversation shifted to: ‘What risks are we proactively addressing before next month-end?’

That was a turning point.

What I Learned

For a long time, I believed strong SLA governance meant strong AMS. I defended green dashboards in meetings. In hindsight, I was defending the wrong metric.

Ticket closure is activity. Stability is outcome.

Running SAP AMS like a product means:

  • Clear ownership at process level
  • Measuring recurrence, not just response time
  • Prioritising structural fixes over cosmetic closure
  • Accepting short-term delivery pressure to gain long-term stability

It does not require heavy transformation. It requires clarity and discipline.

I have seen this pattern more than once , especially in healthcare and pharma environments where compliance and month-end accuracy are critical.

The lesson for me was simple.

If the same issue returns, the system is telling you something.
Dashboards may stay green. But business memory does not reset every month.

That experience fundamentally changed how I lead AMS today.

If you are running SAP AMS in a post-S/4HANA environment, I would be curious:

Are you measuring how fast tickets close or how rarely they return?

 

Conclusion

Stabilisation after S/4HANA go-live is not only about meeting SLAs. It is about reducing recurrence and rebuilding business confidence. By shifting focus from ticket closure to process-level accountability, we were able to improve stability in a measurable way. For organisations running SAP AMS today, the real question is not how fast incidents close, but whether the same ones continue to return.

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone: Tile Opens(content federation) but App Fails with ‘IP Address Cannot Be Found
entry.content:
In my SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition) environment, I faced an issue where a dynamic tile loaded successfully through content federation, but clicking the tile caused the application to fail with a browser error:

ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED – The IP address of the server could not be found

The failing URL always contained a very long auto‑generated hostname.

 

Interestingly, the dynamic tile itself worked correctly (e.g., OData count displayed), but navigating to the corresponding SAP Fiori app consistently failed. This clearly indicated that the issue was not with backend connectivity or destinations, but with the content runtime URL generated by Work Zone.
 
Please refer the screenshots.

entry.title: How ATP & Advanced ATP (aATP) Supercharge the SAP CPQ Sales Quotation Process
entry.content:
When sales teams prepare quotes, one of the biggest challenges is determining accurate product availability and realistic delivery dates. SAP CPQ excels at configuration, pricing, and quote generation, but to promise delivery with confidence, it must rely on real-time inventory and supply chain insights.

That is exactly where SAP ATP (Available‑to‑Promise) and SAP aATP (Advanced Available‑to‑Promise) become powerful extensions during Sales process purely designed and developed custom Iflow (Not available from SAP CPQ Standard Integration Packages).

This blog explores how ATP/aATP works, how it integrates with SAP CPQ and S/4HANA, and the tangible benefits it brings to the sales quotation cycle.

Available Standard APIs for S/4 HANA Public/Private/Onprem

Standard APIsS/4 HANA Public CloudS/4 HANA OnPrem(Private Cloud)
Classical ATPProduct Availability InfoProduct Availability Info
 Basic Product Availability Info
Basic Product Availability Info
 Advanced Backorder Processing Run  
Advanced ATPAdvanced ATP Check Advanced ATP Check
 Supply Availability Protection Plan  
 Product Allocation Object  
 Product Substitution Control
 
 Product Substitution Group
 
 Plant Substitution Control  
 Storage Location Substitution
 
 Storage Location Substitution Exclusion  
 Storage Location Substitution Group  

1. What Is ATP and aATP?

ATP – Classic Availability Check

ATP checks evaluate whether requested materials are available on a given date based on stock, incoming supply, and existing commitments. Classic ATP is embedded in SAP ERP and S/4HANA

aATP – Advanced, Intelligent Availability Checking

Advanced ATP in S/4HANA brings enhanced capabilities, including:

  • Product Availability Check (PAC)
  • Backorder Processing (BOP)
  • Product Allocation (PAL)
  • Alternative-Based Confirmation (ABC)
    These provide intelligent, real-time confirmations considering multiple locations, constraints, and rules

2. Why ATP/aATP Matters for SAP CPQ

SAP CPQ can generate highly accurate quotes, but without visibility into inventory or supply constraints, quotes may overpromise and damage customer trust. ATP/aATP ensures:

  • Real-time material availability during quoting
  • Accurate delivery dates
  • Feasible commitments, even with complex configurations
  • Optimized supply allocation for high-value customers

As SAP CPQ integrates natively with SAP S/4HANA via custom development, sales reps can access availability checks via APIs such as API_AVAIL_TO_PROMISE_CHECK or during quotation-to-sales-order conversion.

3. How ATP/aATP Integrates with SAP CPQ

3.1 Custom Development SAP CPQ–S/4HANA Integration

SAP provides predefined CPQ integration with S/4HANA via BTP Cloud Integration, enabling exchange of configuration, pricing, and master data. A custom Iflow needs to be designed, developed and to be tested to your business requirments that requires ATP or aATP during the Sales Quotation Process before the Sales Order/Solution Quotation/Solution Order is been triggerred

Once CPQ creates a quote and the user triggers an ATP check (either directly or when converting the quote to an order), S/4HANA performs:

  • Real-time availability check
  • Temporary quantity assignment (TQA)
  • Confirmation simulation
    These ATP checks help prevent false confirmations and maintain accuracy in early phase quotations.

3.2 ATP Simulation in the CPQ Process

Sales Order supports ATP simulation via OData APIs, allowing CPQ to request real-time availability before final order creation. 

3.3 Variant Configuration & ATP

When quotes involve configurable products, the integration leverages SAP Variant Configuration and Pricing, ensuring ATP checks consider(This is not part of the Custom Iflow Development):

  • Component availability
  • Alternative sourcing options
  • Production and supply constraints

4. Benefits of Integrating ATP/aATP with SAP CPQ

4.1 Accurate, Real-Time Quotes

aATP delivers real-time confirmations using S/4HANA’s in-memory performance, ensuring CPQ quotes reflect live supply chain data.

4.2 Reduced Order Rejections

By validating availability early, CPQ avoids orders failing later due to material shortages or unrealistic dates.

4.3 Improved Customer Commitment Accuracy

With ABC, BOP, and PAL, CPQ can provide delivery promises aligned with supply chain realities, reducing missed delivery windows.

4.5 Enhanced Sales Productivity

Sales reps can generate quotes more confidently without waiting for manual supply chain consultations.
SAP CPQ already accelerates complex quote creation, and ATP enhances accuracy

4.4 Optimized Fulfillment Decisions

aATP can automatically evaluate multiple warehouses, alternative plants, or substitute products before CPQ confirms availability.

Conclusion

SAP ATP/aATP transforms SAP CPQ from a powerful quoting tool into an end-to-end, availability-aware commitment system.
By combining CPQ’s configuration and pricing intelligence with real-time supply checks, organizations achieve:

  • Faster, more accurate quotations
  • Stronger order reliability
  • Greater customer satisfaction
  • Optimized supply chain execution

In a world where customers expect instant, accurate promises, integrating ATP/aATP with SAP CPQ is no longer optional—it’s a competitive necessity.

entry.title: Tips while configuring SAP Asset accounting and Data upload.
entry.content:
Recently i got a chance to work in 1 project where my role was of Asset Data migration lead. I had to monitor the uploads of the Asset Data.  In each project i learn something and i document it for future reference. I convert that document into blog and share with my community so that they also can benefit from it. 

Disclaimer - This blog does not reflect my expertise but my experience. I received lot of support from my Senior and Junior team members and Data migration was successful. 

Alone and we will sink, collectively and we will sail through. 

So, let's begin.

 

  • Negative values allowed for depreciation area.

When you configure asset accounting, Chart of Depreciation area and depreciation area is must. Most important point while configuring Depreciation areas is to enable it for negative values. Once you do this, assets will be allowed for negative posting. 

During our data upload, we uploaded few assets incorrectly. We uploaded without negative values tick mark, and this was creating a problem. System was not allowed to reverse the asset document in AB08. Now all assets’ masters were enabled for negative postings, and it allowed them to reverse, except 1 sub-number asset.

Asset sub-number was deriving values from Main Asset. So, when we check negative value box in Main number, sub number asset error message was reflecting. When we try to check negative value box in Sub Number asset, Main asset error message was reflected.

So always mark negative values for all depreciation areas to avoid such problems.

 

  • Real and derived areas should have same values.

When you configure the depreciation area, make sure all values in Real areas and Derived areas are similar.  For e.g. 01 and 30. 01 is Real and 30 is derived from 01. In 01, if Acquisition values are ALL VALUES ALLOWED, then 30 should have same values. I know this is very simple and basic, but simples’ things tend to be missed more. So, check all this in detail.

 

  • Sub number asset derived field should be editable.

Generally, we derive values in sub number from Main Asset number, and this is valid too. But those fields are non-editable, and this may create last minute problems. As mentioned above, we tried to reverse the Asset and Sub Asset, but it was not allowed to reverse due to negative postings. Also, it was not allowed to save both the assets after checking Negative values Box.

Reason, in Asset main number, depreciation key was 0001 and in sub asset number it was 0001. We wanted to change to 0002 but field was not modified. If field would be editable, this problem would have been mitigated.

Go to AO21. Sbno. Should be ticked.

 

  • All Depreciation area in asset master

Depreciation area configured in OADB should reflect in Asset master in AS03. If it is not reflected, it is a concern. I know this is basic, but this can be missed.  Check for all company codes why this depreciation area is not coming. Because moving transport at the last moment may create lot of problems. If you do not see the depreciation area, go to OAYZ and check if it is deactivated. If yes, activate.

Note : In ECC, there was AFAN tcode to activate all depreciation area but I could not found this in S/4. We need to activate one by one for each Asset Class.

 

  • Real Depreciation Area Tick Mark.

When configuration is done, check Real area tick mark is made only for Real Depreciation Area. For derived depreciation area there should be no tick mark. If you try to change this at later point of time, this will create problem. So do check this beforehand.

 

  • Data templates should have negative values allowed.

When data is uploaded, instruct your Data team to put X in negative values box for all Asset master’s file. With this, your asset master is now enabled for negative postings. This will help you at the time of reversal of asset.

 

  • Bot should be configured for exact date.

This could be a big issue for Data Migration team. For e.g. extraction date is 31.12.2025. so you should get data as on 31.12.2025. Cumulative value should have data, but depreciation posted and Transaction data should be blank.

Now what happens, BOT is not configured/customized to extract data for exact date, and this could be a problem.  For e.g. you are on 18-02-2026. Data to be extracted as on 31.12.2025 but BOT is not customized to extract data as on 31.12.2025. So, they will extract 3 sheets for you.

  1. Cumulative value
  2. Depreciation posted
  3. Transaction data.

Data team will spend time in collating above information and there is a big risk, like data mismatch.

 

  •      Data Re-upload

Always be ready for this. Sometimes 

  1. cost center is incorrect or
  2. sub number field is not editable or
  3. Depreciation key is incorrect or
  4. Special Depreciation is not reflecting in AW01N or Asset History sheet. 
  5. ......Could be many more.

 

  •     Data Verification.

Sometime client complaints about data is incorrect. They are completely mismatching and you start spending time in reconciliation. At the end you find all data upload is correct and there is no error. So where is the error? 

Ask the user which file you have referred? Extraction file or converted file? Sometimes they reconcile data with the Extracted file from legacy system and not the converted file. This seems very simple, but this will take away lot of time.

 

  •    Data conversion (Most Important)

Never take responsibility of file conversion from the client. Tell client to convert the raw data to uploaded data and share with us to upload. If you take this responsibility, this will create many problems like - 

  1. Time management
  2. Erroneous data
  3. Accountable and responsible.
  4. ......could be many more.

 

  •     Access to legacy system

Make sure you have access to legacy system if you are responsible for data conversion. This will help you verify the data upfront with extracted file.  If you do not have access, you will have dependency upon user to extract data from legacy system. This is very tiring and cumbersome.

So to conclude, there are many more things in data migration which need to be taken care. Above points are one which i fell are important, so shared with you all.

Hope you liked this blog.

Regards

CA Zunaid Hingora

entry.title: How Do I Activate a Situation Type in the Extended Framework?
entry.content:
Situation Handling notifies users about issues requiring their attention. You may choose SAP delivered situation templates or design your own use cases. For the extended framework for Situation Handling, we often discussed how you can create custom situation templates. The framework comes also with SAP delivered use cases that you can easily activate.

This blog post explains how you can activate situation types from SAP delivered situation templates with the extended framework for Situation Handling. It has been available since SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2202 and SAP S/4HANA 2021 FPS02.

 

SAP Delivered Use Cases

Looking for Situation Handling use cases? Visit SAP Help and navigate to Situation Handling, then choose Use Cases. You can narrow your results by filtering for Situation Template Type (either extended object-based or message-based), area, and topic. To learn more about a specific use case, simply check the link in the Situation Template column for a detailed description.

Use Case

For illustration purposes we use the situation template Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Purchasers get a heads-up for purchase contracts that are about to expire.

 

Activation Overview for 2SL and 3SL

Before discussing the activation steps in detail, let me point out the differences between system landscapes and their specific transport paths.

In the extended framework for Situation Handling, you create a situation type from a situation template. Situation templates are only visible in the quality system of a 2-system landscape (2SL) and the development system of a  3-system-landscape (3SL). The situation type needs to be transported to the production system. The transport paths differ between 2SL and 3SL.

Transport the Situation Type in 2SL

In a 2-system-landscape, you transport the situation type from the quality system to the production system.

  • In the quality system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Export the well-defined situation type
  • In the production system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between quality and production system you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 2-System-Landscape

Transport the Situation Type in 3SL

In a 3-system-landscape, transports are required from the development system to both the test and production system. In a 3SL environment you may also have different tenants for development, customizing, and parallel customizing that need to be considered as well.

  • On the customizing tenant in the development system you
    • Create a situation type from a situation template
    • Export the situation type
  • On the test tenant in the test system you
    • Import the situation type
    • Since the data usually differs between systems you need to enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Test if the configuration alerts the right users about the situation
    • Forward the well-defined situation type
  • On the production tenant in the production system you
    • Import the forwarded situation type
    • Enter the appropriate values for the condition filters
    • Enable the situation type

Transports in 3-System-Landscape

Detailed Activation of a Situation Type in 3SL

Now let’s check the detailed steps for a sample use case in a 3-system-landscape as it is more comprehensive. In 2SL the export / import mechanism resembles the one from development system to test system in 3SL.

Depending on your system setting, the transports may also be processed automatically. For optimal clarity, the blog post discusses the transport steps through manual triggers.

Development System

Create a Situation Type from a Situation Template

Log-in as business process configuration expert on the customizing tenant in the development system. Open the Manage Situation Types – Extended app where you see a list of SAP delivered templates. Use the search option to find the template for our use case Expiration of Purchase Contracts (PROC_ABOUT_TO_EXPIRE_CTR). Select the template and an object page opens. In the header, select Create Situation Type.

Create situation type from situation template

A new page opens with the situation type that contains all information from the situation template. You just need to enter an ID, Name and Description. The type is configured and ready to use. Depending on your requirements you could adapt the settings, for example, by adding filter values for the conditions, changing texts or rescheduling the batch job.

I recommend monitoring the instances to see how situations are handled.

Monitor situations

Let’s check the relevant settings and understand how the configuration works.
